title: Chapter 31: The Return
content: Six months.

Six months since the Choice, and I'm on the 14:07 from Cork to Dublin, watching Ireland smear itself across the window in fifty shades of green I couldn't have named before March. The fields are ridiculous — impossibly rolling, absurdly emerald, like someone cranked the saturation on real life and forgot to turn it back down. Sheep dot the hills like punctuation marks in a language I'm still learning to read.
My reflection ghosts against the glass. I look at her — at me — and wonder if Mika will see the same thing I do.

This is the longest I've gone without seeing Mika Torres since we roomed together sophomore year, since she taught me how to use a curling iron without burning my ear and I taught her how to write a conclusion paragraph that didn't sound like a hostage note. Six months of FaceTime and voice notes and one very long email about the legal implications of international cohabitation that Mika sent at 3 AM with the subject line "I AM YOUR FRIEND AND YOUR LAWYER AND I AM VOLUNTEERING BOTH."
But seeing someone through a screen is like looking at the moon through a telescope — you get the topography, not the gravity.

The train rattles through a tunnel and my reflection sharpens. Dark hair longer now, past my shoulders. Blush I actually remember to wear. The faint crease between my brows that used to live there permanently — gone. Or at least quieter.

I'm not nervous about Mika. Mika is a fixed point; she'll find me anywhere. I'm nervous about who I'll be when Mika sees me. Whether the person I've become these last six months will hold up under her assessment. Because Mika doesn't do gentle landings. Mika does impact analysis.
The train sighs into Heuston Station. I grab my bag, shoulder through the crowd, step out into Dublin air that tastes like rain that's thinking about happening.

Temple Bar is exactly what you'd expect on a Saturday evening — tourists with pints, buskers with acoustic guitars, hen parties with feather boas trailing like conquered flags. The noise hits me in waves: a fiddle spilling from one doorway, laughter crashing from another, the percussive thump of combat boots on cobblestone ahead.
Combat boots.

Mika is leaning against the pub's stone façade, arms crossed, undercut sharp enough to cut the wind. She's wearing her leather jacket — the one she's had since we were twenty, the one with the patch that says BAD INFLUENCE stitched crookedly on the shoulder. She looks exactly the same. Same dark eyes, same sharp jaw, same "I could destroy you and you'd thank me" energy radiating off her like heat from tarmac.

She sees me and her face does something complicated. Recognition first — easy, reflexive. Then relief — her shoulders drop half an inch. Then assessment — her eyes sweep me from head to toe, cataloguing changes, measuring the distance between the Avery she last held and the Avery standing in front of her now.
"You look different," she says.
"Good different or bad different?"
"Real different."

I don't know what I expected her to say, but it wasn't that. It wasn't a word that lands like both a diagnosis and a benediction. Real. As if everything before was a simulation. As if she noticed the performance I didn't even know I was running.

She kicks off the wall and pulls me into a hug — tight, brief, fierce. She smells like leather and coconut shampoo and the sharp citrus of whatever she spritzed on her neck. My arms go around her and I feel the familiar architecture of her — bird-bone shoulders, steel spine.
"Come on," she says, releasing me. "I've already got a booth."

The pub is dark wood and stained glass and the yeasty malt-smell of beer being pulled. We settle into a corner booth — Mika with her back to the wall, because of course — and she orders two pints of Guinness without asking, because some things don't change and thank God for that.

The pints arrive with their creamy heads and I take a sip and the taste blooms across my tongue — bitter and roasty and cold and somehow like home, even though this isn't my home, not yet, not officially, but maybe the way a place becomes home isn't through paperwork but through pints with your person in corner booths on Saturday nights.
"So," Mika says, setting her glass down. "The Avery Chen. In Dublin. On purpose."
"On purpose," I confirm.
"Weird."
"Very."
She grins. It's the grin that got us kicked out of that bar in the East Village in 2019. It's the grin that means she's happy and won't say it directly, so she'll weaponize the moment instead.

And I realize — this is different. For the first time in years, I don't feel like I'm performing friendship. I'm not curating my side of the conversation, not managing Mika's expectations, not reflexively editing myself to the version of Avery she'd find most palatable. I'm just sitting here. Being her friend. Being someone's friend the way breathing is just being alive — automatic, essential, no thought required.
Mika must feel it too, because something in her expression softens. Just a fraction. A crack in the armor where light gets in.

"Okay," she says. "Business first." She pulls her phone out, swipes through something. "Your sublet ended. Your stuff is in storage — I handled the logistics, you owe me exactly one nice dinner and unlimited access to whatever Irish whiskey Julian's got hidden away. HR at Sterling called twice. They want to know if you're coming back."
The question lands between us like a coin dropped on the bar. Heads: New York. Tails: here.
Is she?

I consider it. Not the way I used to — not the frantic calculus of FOMO and opportunity cost and what-if-I'm-making-the-biggest-mistake-of-my-life that used to short-circuit my brain every time I had to choose anything. I consider it the way you consider a menu at a restaurant where you've already eaten. The options are there. Some of them are good. But you're full.

I have skills. I have a career — had one, and could have one again, in a glass tower where the air tastes filtered and the hours taste like burnout. I have — had — a life in New York. A Murray Hill apartment I never loved. A commute I hated. A man who believed the impossible before he even knew it was possible.

But that world is gone. Not just the timelines — not just the Fork app and its branching paths and the versions of reality where different Choices led to different disasters. The version of myself who belonged in that world. She's gone too. Not dead — evolved. Shed like skin that no longer fits.
"I'm not going back to Sterling," I say.
Mika waits. She's a lawyer. She knows when the sentence isn't finished.
"But I might go back to New York. Someday. To visit. Not to live."
Mika nods slowly. The way she nods when a closing argument lands. "That sounds like a decision someone who's not terrified would make."
"Because I'm not terrified anymore."

The words come out before I can examine them, and then they're just — true. Hanging in the pub air between us, solid as the Guinness in my hand. First time I've said it. First time I've meant it, down to the marrow, without the asterisk of but what if, without the fine print of conditions and contingencies.
Mika picks up her pint. "To not being terrified."
We clink glasses. The sound is small and perfect.
"Okay," she says, wiping foam from her lip. "Now tell me about him."
"Julian?"
"No, the other man you uprooted your entire life for. Yes, Julian."

I take a breath. This is the part where I should gush — where old Avery would've gushed, would've built a monument out of specifics, cataloguing every eyelash and micro-expression until the word "love" got buried under the rubble of overanalysis.

But that's not how you describe someone you've chosen. You describe them the way you describe your own heartbeat — quietly, certainly, with the settled warmth of something that doesn't need spectacle to be real.
"He's kind in a way that costs him something," I say. "He doesn't know how to be any other way."
Mika stares at me. Then she leans back against the booth and lets out a low whistle. "That's the hottest thing you've ever said about a man."
"It's true."

"I know. That's why it's hot." She takes a long pull of her pint. "The Avery I knew used to describe guys like they were LinkedIn profiles. 'He's ambitious. He's got a five-year plan. He's really into Peloton.' And now you're out here talking about a man like he's a — a moral event horizon."
I laugh because she's right. Because the Avery who dated men with five-year plans was a woman with a five-year plan, and every plan was a leash she put on herself.

"He plays guitar again," I add. "He stopped for a while — years. And now he plays every night. Sometimes I find him in the kitchen at midnight, just picking out chords like he's having a conversation with someone I can't see. And he always looks up when I come in. Like I'm the answer to whatever question the song was asking."
Mika's eyes are bright. She's not crying. Mika Torres doesn't cry in pubs. But she's paying attention the way she pays attention in a deposition — mining for truth beneath the surface.
"He sounds good, Ave."
"He is good."
"No — you sound good. With him. Like you fit."
I do. I fit. Not the way puzzle pieces fit — that's too neat, too static. The way a hand fits in another hand. Adjustable. Warm. Chosen every time.

We leave the pub when the crowd gets loud enough to make conversation feel like shouting across a canyon. Mika tosses cash on the table — she insists, says I can get the next round, and there will be a next round, this is not negotiable — and we step out into the blue hour, that liminal space between afternoon and evening where Dublin goes soft and gold and the streetlamps are just starting to blink awake.

We walk along the Liffey. The water catches the last of the sunset and throws it back in broken ribbons of copper and rose. The air smells like the river — mineral and alive — and like chips from a nearby vendor, and like the rain that's still thinking about happening, hovering in the clouds like a promise that might not keep itself.
"So," Mika says, her combat boots clicking against the path. "Remember when you couldn't choose a cereal at the grocery store?"
I groan. "We are not doing this."
"We are absolutely doing this. You once stood in the cereal aisle for forty-five minutes. I timed you. You were comparing fiber content between two brands of Cheerios."
"They had different calcium percentages!"
"Avery." She stops walking. Turns to face me, the sunset painting half her face gold, the other half in shadow. "You once texted me a pros-and-cons list about whether to get bangs."
"I looked terrible with bangs—"

"My point." She steps closer. "My point is: you couldn't choose a cereal. You couldn't choose a haircut. You couldn't choose a train ticket without calculating the opportunity cost of every other train you weren't taking. And now you've chosen a whole life. In Ireland. With a man who was on the run three months ago."
I elbow her — my arm connecting with the leather of her jacket, the impact travelling up to my elbow, satisfying and familiar. "He's not on the run anymore."
"No," Mika says, and her voice shifts into something quieter, something that holds the weight of all the months between us. "He's with you. That's much more dangerous."

She's smiling when she says it, and I'm smiling, and we're both laughing — laughing the way you laugh when something is true and ridiculous and exactly right, the way you laugh with the person who knew you when you were a mess and loves you still, not because you've changed but because you were always worth loving even when you couldn't see it.
It's the best sound in any world. Any timeline. Any version of reality that ever was or will be. This laugh, this river, this sky.
My phone lights up in my pocket.

I almost ignore it. I want to ignore it — I'm here, I'm present, I'm with Mika in a city I chose, and the world can wait. But the screen glows through the fabric of my jeans, insistent, and old instincts die hard.

I pull it out. Not the Fork app. That's gone — dissolved when the timelines collapsed, vanished from my phone along with every other trace, like a fever dream breaking at dawn. Just an email. From Eamon.
Subject line: "It's over."
My thumb hovers. My heart does something complicated — not fear, not anymore, but the reflex of it, the muscle memory of bracing.
I open it.

*It's over. Osprey dissolved. The commanding officer confessed — full statement, on the record. Julian's name has been formally cleared. All charges dropped, all records expunged. He's free, Avery. Tell him he's free.*
I show Mika. No words. Just the phone, the screen, the truth of it in pixels.
Mika reads. Her eyes move left to right, left to right. When she finishes, she looks up.
"So he's free."

I nod. My throat is tight. The Liffey keeps moving beside us, indifferent to the magnitude of this moment, which is maybe the kindest thing a river can do — just keep flowing, like the world hasn't changed, even when it has.
"They're both free," Mika says.
And she means both of us.

She means Julian — finally, officially, legally free of the shadow that chased him across countries and timelines and into my arms. Free of the dishonour that was never his, the accusations that clung to him like smoke even when there was no fire.

And she means me. Free of the Fork. Free of the tyranny of infinite Choice. Free of the version of myself who was so paralyzed by the possibility of choosing wrong that she almost chose nothing at all.

The sunset catches the water one last time before the clouds swallow it. The rain decides to happen — soft, barely there, the kind of Irish rain that's more of a suggestion than a statement. It touches my face. It tastes clean.
I put my phone away.
"Come on," I say to Mika. "I owe you dinner."
"You owe me a lot more than dinner."
"That too."

We walk. The rain falls. The Liffey flows. Somewhere in Cork, in the small house with the guitar in the kitchen and the garden that's starting to bloom and the man who waited six years for his name to be cleared, Julian is probably making tea. Probably looking out the window at the same sky. Probably not knowing yet that the world just shifted under his feet for the last time.
I'll tell him when I get home.
Home. Not an apartment in Murray Hill. Not a sublet. Not a life someone chose for me. Home — a place I chose, with a person I choose, every single day that I get to choose.

And I do get to choose. That's the part I'm still getting used to. Not the weight of it — I've always felt the weight. The freedom of it. The fact that choosing wrong won't end the world. Won't end any world.

The only world that matters is this one. Right here. Rain on my face, Mika at my side, a cleared name waiting in Cork, and the whole long stretch of a life I finally get to live without looking over my shoulder.
We're free.
Both of us.
Finally.
